The international alliance said id

The central body endorsed the ar
bitration law now pending before
the state legislature. The bills pro-
viding for damages in case of acct
dents to employees Of railway and
traction lines, the payment of labor
in lawful money or negotiable paper
good for its face value, and an act
to compel aliens who have not de-
clared their intention to become
citizens to pay tuition in the
schools of S. per month. This last
law, it was stated, was aimed at the
Japanese and was introduced by
Hutchinson, of this county. The

men were patronizing the Mohler
barber college and that they expect
ed them to keep away from that
institution in the future.


E. A. Cooney, of the steam en-
gineers, in short speech advocated
the raising of the dues paid by the


unions into the central body. He
said at the present time the or
ganization had barely enough


money on hand to pay the actual


rdunning expenses of the organiza-
ton and he advocated the raising
Of a fund for eventualities in case
Of trouble. He pointed out that the


central body should have fund for
the purpose of securing legal adj
vice when necessary or to aid the
young and dependent unions when
they are involved in trouble and
need financial assistance.


The troubles of the street car
men was referred to the executive
committee for whatever action


might be necessary in case strike
took place before another meeting


of the central body.
